title: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors:
Neal K. Lakdawala,

Lynne Warner Stevenson, Joseph Loscalzo

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS
items.title: Understanding the Phases of Viral Myocarditis Pathogenesis
items.content: Viral myocarditis pathogenesis is traditionally described in three phases, primarily studied using murine models with enteroviruses. Phase I involves direct viral invasion and cellular damage, while Phase II is characterized by an immune response to infection without direct viral injury, often leading to 'virally triggered' myocarditis. The third phase may progress to chronic cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Phase I: Direct myocardial invasion (e.g., coxsackie B virus) with potential genetic predisposition enhancing cellular entry.
Phase II: Immune response involving macrophage activation and T- and B-cell expansion, often associated with respiratory viruses like adenovirus, influenza, and COVID-19.
Phase III: Progression to chronic cardiomyopathy is rare in adults; MRI patterns previously linked to viral myocarditis are also seen in genetic cardiomyopathies.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS
items.title: Role of Genetic Variants in Inflammatory Myocarditis
items.content: Genetic predisposition plays a significant role in inflammatory myocarditis, with nearly one in five patients harboring genetic variants considered pathogenic for cardiomyopathy. This suggests that both genetic factors and immune responses contribute to myocyte pathology.
keyPoints: Approximately 20% of patients with inflammatory myocarditis have genetic variants associated with cardiomyopathy.
Genetic predisposition may influence the inflammatory response and susceptibility to myocardial injury.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS
items.title: Implications of MRI Findings in Cardiomyopathy Diagnosis
items.content: Intramyocardial late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI, once considered indicative of prior viral myocarditis, is now recognized as common in genetic cardiomyopathies as well. This highlights the need for careful interpretation of imaging findings.
keyPoints: Late gadolinium enhancement on MRI was previously linked to past viral myocarditis but is also seen in genetic cardiomyopathy cases.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.title: Recognizing Acute Myocarditis Presentation
items.content: Acute myocarditis often presents with chest pain (>80% of patients), dyspnea, or arrhythmias. Prodromal symptoms resembling influenza may precede the condition by days to weeks. Fever is present in over half of cases.
keyPoints: Occurs more commonly in men aged 30-45 years and women >45 years
Chest pain occurs in >80% of patients
Fever present in >50% of cases
Arrhythmias can lead to sudden cardiac death (3–10%)
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing Acute Myocarditis: Criteria and Imaging
items.content: Probable myocarditis is diagnosed with new symptoms plus elevated troponin, ECG changes, or imaging showing decreased LVEF. Definite diagnosis can be non-invasively confirmed with MRI evidence of myocardial edema and injury without other causes.
keyPoints: Probable myocarditis: New symptom + elevated troponin/ECG/imaging
Definite myocarditis: Elevated troponin + MRI findings (edema, nonischemic injury)
LVEF <0.50 in ~25% of cases
Endomyocardial biopsy indicated for ventricular arrhythmias or systemic autoimmune disease
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Management of Acute Myocarditis Based on Risk Stratification
items.content: Acute myocarditis can be categorized into uncomplicated and complicated presentations. Uncomplicated cases, characterized by LVEF ≥ 0.50 without ventricular arrhythmias or circulatory shock, generally have a good prognosis with spontaneous recovery in about three-fourths of patients. These patients often do not require specific therapies beyond diuretics and NSAIDs for chest pain. In contrast, complicated presentations carry a higher risk, with ~18% mortality or heart transplantation within 5 years.
keyPoints: Uncomplicated acute myocarditis: LVEF ≥ 0.50
Spontaneous recovery in three-fourths of patients without specific therapies other than diuretics and NSAIDs
~18% mortality or heart transplantation in complicated cases over 5 years
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Chronic Myocarditis and Cardiomyopathy: Diagnostic Considerations
items.content: Patients with reduced LVEF during acute myocarditis are at risk of developing chronic cardiomyopathy. Persistent elevation of troponin or unresolved LVEF in initial months post-diagnosis warrants further evaluation using CMRi or endomyocardial biopsy to assess ongoing inflammation and fibrosis. Chronic inflammatory disease may also be present in patients with gradual onset dilated cardiomyopathy without prior acute myocarditis, though immunosuppression has not shown convincing benefits.
keyPoints: Reduced LVEF at presentation increases risk of chronic cardiomyopathy
Persistent troponin elevation or unresolved LVEF indicates need for CMRi or biopsy
Immunosuppression in chronic nonspecific lymphocytic myocarditis lacks convincing benefit
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.title: COVID-19 Myocarditis: Incidence and Mechanisms
items.content: COVID-19, an RNA virus, has been associated with significant cardiac involvement in hospitalized patients with severe disease. The myocarditis diagnosis is primarily based on clinical findings and elevated enzymes due to the rarity of biopsies. During the pandemic, myocarditis incidence increased dramatically.
keyPoints: Estimated rate of myocarditis during COVID-19: 150/100,000 people
15-fold increase over pre-COVID incidence
Most common in young men under age 40
Potential mechanisms: cytokine storm, activated T cells and macrophages, molecular mimicry, extracellular vesicles with viral RNA
Prothrombotic endotheliopathy contributing to myocardial ischemia and stroke
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · SPECIFIC CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.title: HIV-Associated Cardiomyopathy: Incidence and Contributing Factors
items.content: HIV is associated with a dilated cardiomyopathy incidence of 1–2%, which has declined due to antiretroviral therapy. Cardiomyopathy can also result from other viruses like cytomegalovirus, hepatitis C, or as a side effect of antiviral drugs.
keyPoints: Dilated cardiomyopathy incidence in HIV: 1–2%
Decline due to highly active antiretroviral therapy
Other contributing factors: associated viruses (cytomegalovirus, hepatitis C), drug hypersensitivity
Complications may include pericardial effusions and pulmonary hypertension
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · SPECIFIC CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.title: Chagas' Disease: A Leading Parasitic Cause of Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Chagas' disease, caused by T. cruzi, is a significant parasitic cause of cardiomyopathy worldwide. It primarily affects rural areas in South and Central America but has been identified in non-endemic regions due to migration.
keyPoints: Third most common parasitic infection globally
Prevalence decreased from 18 million (1990) to 6 million
>300,000 infected residents in the U.S., some acquired locally
5-10% of initial infections present with acute myocarditis
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Chagas' Myocarditis
items.content: For patients from endemic regions or those presenting with suggestive cardiac symptoms, consider T. cruzi infection. PCR is the most sensitive test for acute myocarditis; serologic tests are used in chronic phases.
keyPoints: Consider T. cruzi in evaluation of acute myocarditis or cardiomyopathy
PCR is most sensitive for acute phase detection
Serologic tests needed for chronic phase diagnosis
Antiparasitic treatment recommended for acute myocarditis and certain indeterminate phase patients
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.title: Trichinellosis: Rare Cardiac Involvement in a Nematode Infection
items.content: Trichinellosis, caused by Trichinella nematodes, primarily affects skeletal muscles. Myocardial involvement is rare and usually due to an eosinophilic inflammatory response.
keyPoints: Caused by ingestion of undercooked meat with larvae
Clinical heart failure from myocardial invasion is rare
Diagnosis supported by serologies and eosinophilia
Treatment includes anthelmintics and glucocorticoids for severe inflammation
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.title: Diphtheria and Cardiac Involvement
items.content: Diphtheria, caused by Corynebacterium diphtheriae, can lead to myocarditis due to its toxin interfering with protein synthesis in the heart. This cardiac involvement is a significant cause of mortality from the infection.
keyPoints: Cardiac involvement is the most common cause of death from diphtheria.
Diagnosis involves pharyngeal bacterial culture on special media and toxin assay.
Clinical suspicion warrants immediate administration of antitoxin, prioritized over antibiotics.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.title: Lyme Disease and Cardiac Manifestations
items.content: Lyme disease, caused by Borrelia burgdorferi, can lead to myocarditis. It typically presents with arthritis and conduction system issues that resolve within 1–2 weeks of antibiotic treatment.
keyPoints: Lyme carditis often resolves quickly with antibiotics.
Rarely implicated in chronic heart failure.
Treated effectively with doxycycline.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Cardiac Sarcoidosis in Heart Failure and Arrhythmias
items.content: Cardiac sarcoidosis should be considered when heart failure or ventricular tachyarrhythmias occur without coronary artery disease. Right bundle branch block is a key indicator, as it's uncommon with other cardiomyopathies.
keyPoints: Right bundle branch block raises suspicion for cardiac sarcoidosis.
Sarcoidosis may present with rapid-onset heart failure and ventricular tachyarrhythmias.
Ventricular size can be near normal, described as 'nondilated' or 'minimally dilated cardiomyopathy.'
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Cardiac Sarcoidosis
items.content: Diagnosis is facilitated by biopsy-proven sarcoidosis in other organs. Imaging techniques like MRI and PET can identify fibrosis patterns and active lesions, respectively.
keyPoints: MRI shows late gadolinium enhancement not compatible with myocardial infarction.
PET highlights glucose-avid sarcoid lesions.
Biopsy is often necessary when metabolically active adenopathy is detected.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.title: Managing Cardiac Sarcoidosis with Immunosuppression
items.content: Initial treatment often involves glucocorticoids, starting at lower doses than traditionally used. Steroid-sparing agents and immunomodulators may be added for long-term management.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids are initiated at 30–40 mg daily or less.
Steroid-sparing agents like methotrexate or mycophenolate can be used early.
Treatment impact is more apparent on arrhythmias than left ventricular dysfunction.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.title: Giant Cell Myocarditis: Acute Presentation and Management
items.content: Giant cell myocarditis presents acutely with rapidly progressive heart failure and tachyarrhythmias. It is more common in older patients compared to those with acute viral myocarditis and often associated with systemic autoimmune disorders. Diagnosis typically requires endomyocardial biopsy, revealing granulomatous lesions with extensive inflammation, sometimes including eosinophilic infiltration. Treatment involves immunosuppression similar to severe transplant rejection protocols, as glucocorticoid therapy alone is rarely effective.
keyPoints: Accounts for 10–20% of biopsy-proven myocarditis
Typically presents acutely with rapidly progressive heart failure and tachyarrhythmias
More often associated with systemic autoimmune disorders
Endomyocardial biopsy reveals granulomatous lesions surrounded by extensive inflammation, often with eosinophilic infiltration
Glucocorticoid therapy alone is rarely effective; combination immunosuppression reduces death or heart transplantation rates
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.title: Eosinophilic Myocarditis: Causes and Diagnostic Considerations
items.content: Eosinophilic myocarditis can cause fulminant myocarditis, often missed without assessing for hypereosinophilia, present in about three-fourths of cases. It may lead to apical thrombi on cardiac imaging and subendocardial fibrosis in chronic cases as seen on MRI. Biopsies show infiltration with lymphocytes, neutrophils, and eosinophils. Causes include hypersensitivity to medications (up to one-third of cases) and chronic parasitic infections, particularly in Mediterranean and African regions. Hypereosinophilic syndrome accounts for 10–15% of cases.
keyPoints: Hypereosinophilia present in about three-fourths of diagnosed cases
May lead to apical thrombi on cardiac imaging; MRI shows inflammation acutely and subendocardial fibrosis chronically
Endomyocardial biopsies show infiltration with lymphocytes, neutrophils, and eosinophils
Hypersensitivity to chronic medications accounts for up to one-third of cases
Chronic parasitic infection is a cause in Mediterranean and African countries
10–15% of cases due to hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES)
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.question: Which infectious agent is most often associated with myocarditis?
items.explanation: Myocarditis is most often associated with viruses and the protozoal disease Trypanosoma cruzi (Chagas' disease).

items.question: Which phase of viral myocarditis involves direct invasion of the myocardium?
items.explanation: Phase I includes direct invasion of the myocardium, as defined by infection with coxsackie B virus.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS
items.question: What is a potential cause of myocardial injury in the absence of ongoing viral infection?
options: Molecular mimicry between viral and cardiac antigens
items.explanation: Molecular mimicry between viral and cardiac antigens may cause myocardial injury by autoreactive T cells in the absence of ongoing viral infection.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

options: Intramycardial late gadolinium enhancement on MRI
Presence of eosinophils in the myocardium
items.explanation: The MRI pattern of intramycardial late gadolinium enhancement was previously considered evidence of prior viral myocarditis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.question: Which age group is most commonly affected by acute myocarditis in adults?
items.explanation: Acute myocarditis typically occurs between 30 and 45 years in adults.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the most common symptom of acute myocarditis?
items.explanation: Chest pain is the most common symptom, occurring in over 80% of patients.

items.question: Which diagnostic tool can confirm recent infection with influenza and adenovirus in acute myocarditis?
items.explanation: Respiratory viral panels can confirm recent infection with influenza and adenovirus.

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding patients with uncomplicated acute myocarditis?
options: They generally require high-dose inotropic therapy or mechanical circulatory support.
Spontaneous recovery without specific therapies other than diuretics and nonsteroidal analgesics for chest pain is common.
Routine immunosuppression is recommended for patients with presumed viral etiology.
The majority of these patients undergo heart transplantation within 5 years.
items.explanation: Uncomplicated acute myocarditis often results in spontaneous recovery without specific therapies other than diuretics and nonsteroidal analgesics for chest pain.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: What is the recommended therapy for patients with acute myocarditis who have reduced LVEF?
options: High-dose inotropic therapy or mechanical circulatory support.
Therapies for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).
items.explanation: Patients with reduced LVEF are generally discharged on the recommended therapies for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: What is the significance of anticardiac antibodies and viral genome fragments in chronic cardiomyopathy?
options: They are routinely used in clinical practice to diagnose chronic cardiomyopathy.
Their presence definitively indicates ongoing cardiac injury.
They may be causes of ongoing cardiac injury or neutral bystanders.
They have no significance in the context of chronic cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: Anticardiac antibodies and viral genome fragments are of uncertain significance, as they could be either causes of ongoing cardiac injury or neutral bystanders.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.question: Which of the following viruses is NOT part of the picornavirus family implicated in infectious myocarditis?
items.explanation: Influenza is an RNA virus but not part of the picornavirus family.

items.question: What is a common feature of myocarditis associated with COVID-19?
options: High levels of viral particles in myocardial samples
Increased incidence primarily among elderly patients
Clinical findings and elevated enzymes without biopsy confirmation
items.explanation: Diagnosis is limited to clinical findings and elevated enzymes rather than demonstration of lymphocytic infiltrates.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · SPECIFIC CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.question: Which mechanism has NOT been suggested as a potential cause of cardiac injury in viral myocarditis?
items.explanation: Viral particles were identified but at low levels, making direct pathogenicity uncertain.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · SPECIFIC CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.question: Which of the following is the most common infective cause of cardiomyopathy worldwide?
items.explanation: Chagas' disease is the most common infective cause of cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary determinant of ongoing inflammation and progression to chronic heart failure in Chagas' disease?
options: Silent progression during the indeterminate phase
items.explanation: Persistent parasitemia is recognized as the major determinant of ongoing inflammation and progression to chronic heart failure in Chagas' disease.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: Which test is most sensitive for diagnosing acute myocarditis due to T. cruzi infection?
items.explanation: PCR is the most sensitive test for diagnosing acute myocarditis due to T. cruzi infection.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: For which group of patients with Chagas' disease is antiparasitic treatment more often advocated during the indeterminate phase?
options: Premenopausal women and those on immunosuppression
All patients regardless of age or condition
items.explanation: Antiparasitic treatment is more often advocated for patients <50 years old, premenopausal women, and those on immunosuppression during the indeterminate phase.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: Which organism is responsible for causing diphtheria, and what is its primary cardiac effect?
options: Corynebacterium diphtheriae; interferes with protein synthesis in the heart
Streptococcus pyogenes; causes inflammation of the myocardium
Borrelia burgdorferi; leads to chronic heart failure
Mycobacterium tuberculosis; directly involves the myocardium
items.explanation: Corynebacterium diphtheriae causes diphtheria and its toxin interferes with protein synthesis in the heart.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.question: Which treatment is prioritized over antibiotics for suspected diphtheria?
items.explanation: Specific antitoxin should be administered as soon as possible for suspected diphtheria, with higher priority than antibiotic therapy.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.question: Which tick-borne illness is most commonly associated with Lyme carditis?
items.explanation: Lyme disease, caused by Borrelia burgdorferi, is most commonly associated with Lyme carditis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a common noninfective inflammatory process affecting the myocardium?
items.explanation: The passage identifies sarcoidosis as the most commonly diagnosed noninfective inflammatory process affecting the myocardium.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common initial treatment for cardiac sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The passage states that immunosuppression for sarcoidosis is generally initiated with glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.question: What imaging modality can identify late gadolinium enhancement in cardiac sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that MRI of the heart can identify late gadolinium enhancement in a pattern of fibrosis not compatible with myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.question: Which cardiac feature is uncommon with other cardiomyopathies but should raise suspicion for sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The passage notes that right bundle branch block should raise suspicion for sarcoidosis but is uncommon with other cardiomyopathies.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with granulomatous lesions surrounded by extensive inflammation infiltrate, often with eosinophilic infiltration?
items.explanation: Giant cell myocarditis is characterized by granulomatous lesions with extensive inflammation and eosinophilic infiltration.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of biopsy-proven myocarditis cases does giant cell myocarditis account for?
items.explanation: Giant cell myocarditis accounts for 10–20% of biopsy-proven myocarditis cases.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.question: Which therapy is rarely effective alone but can reduce mortality when combined with other immunosuppression therapies in giant cell myocarditis?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoid therapy alone is rarely effective but can reduce mortality when combined with other immunosuppression therapies in giant cell myocarditis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.statement: Most common viruses inflict cardiac damage directly.
items.explanation: Most common viruses do not appear to inflict cardiac damage directly; early cardiac effects may result primarily from cytokine storm and other nonspecific immune responses.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.statement: Chronic myocardial inflammation is a well-established cause of dilated cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: It is uncertain how often chronic myocardial inflammation contributes to dilated cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · Acute and Chronic Myocarditis | MODELS OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.statement: Viral titers are often helpful to guide initial therapy of acute myocarditis.
items.explanation: Viral titers are not often helpful to guide initial therapy.

items.statement: Eosinophil counts should be checked routinely in cases of suspected acute myocarditis.
items.explanation: Eosinophil counts should be checked because hypereosinophilia is present in most cases of eosinophilic myocarditis.

items.statement: Immunosuppression for nonspecific lymphocytic myocarditis in the chronic setting has demonstrated convincing benefit.
items.explanation: Immunosuppression for nonspecific lymphocytic myocarditis in the chronic setting has not demonstrated convincing benefit.

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: Genetic testing reveals that 20–40% of patients with dilated cardiomyopathy have pathogenic variants sufficient to cause their condition.
items.explanation: The prevalence and nature of genetic variants in patients with dilated cardiomyopathy are similar in acute myocarditis, indicating a significant proportion has pathogenic variants.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.statement: PCR detection of viral genomes in myocardial samples is a definitive diagnostic tool for chronic myocarditis.
items.explanation: Viral genomes are also found in normal control hearts, making their contribution to chronic cardiomyopathy uncertain.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · SPECIFIC CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.statement: The incidence of dilated cardiomyopathy associated with HIV is increasing due to the availability of antiretroviral therapy.
items.explanation: The incidence is declining with the availability of highly active antiretroviral therapy.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · SPECIFIC CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS MYOCARDITIS

items.statement: Clinical heart failure is a common outcome of trichinellosis due to myocardial invasion by larvae.
items.explanation: Clinical heart failure is rare in trichinellosis and attributed to the eosinophilic inflammatory response, not direct myocardial invasion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.statement: Bacterial infections commonly cause heart involvement through abscess formation.
items.explanation: Most bacterial infections rarely involve the heart through direct invasion and abscess formation.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.statement: Tuberculosis rarely involves the myocardium when treated with antibiotics.
items.explanation: Tuberculosis can involve the myocardium directly or through tuberculous pericarditis but rarely does so when treated with antibiotics.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.statement: Whipple's disease primarily affects the cardiovascular system.
items.explanation: The usual manifestations of Whipple's disease are in the gastrointestinal tract, not primarily affecting the cardiovascular system.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 2)

items.statement: Cardiac sarcoidosis often accompanies pulmonary sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that cardiac sarcoidosis often accompanies pulmonary sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Immunosuppression for sarcoidosis is typically initiated with antiviral medications.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that immunosuppression is generally initiated with glucocorticoids, not antiviral medications.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Eosinophilic myocarditis is often associated with hypereosinophilia.
items.explanation: Hypereosinophilia is present in about three-fourths of diagnosed cases of eosinophilic myocarditis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.statement: Most patients with giant cell myocarditis presenting with cardiogenic shock do not require urgent mechanical support or transplantation.
items.explanation: Most patients presenting with cardiogenic shock from giant cell myocarditis progress to need urgent mechanical support or transplantation.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 2)

items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.

items.subtopic: THERAPY AND OUTCOMES | CHRONIC MYOCARDITIS AND CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.assertion: Patients with reduced LVEF at the time of presentation with acute myocarditis are at higher risk of developing chronic cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: When the LVEF does not resolve in the initial months after diagnosis, particularly with persistent elevation of troponin, ongoing inflammation and developing fibrosis may be revealed by CMRi or endomyocardial biopsy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with reduced LVEF at presentation are at higher risk of chronic cardiomyopathy.

items.assertion: Chagas' disease can be transmitted orally.
items.reason: The protozoan T. cruzi is transmitted by the bite of the reduviid bug and occasionally orally.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.assertion: Antiparasitic treatment is recommended for all patients with Chagas' cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: A large multinational trial showed no benefit for benznidazole given to patients with chronic heart failure from Chagas' cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it only applies to a specific group of patients in chronic heart failure.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · OTHER INFECTIOUS CAUSES (part 1)

items.assertion: Diagnosis of cardiac sarcoidosis is easiest in the presence of biopsy-proven sarcoidosis of other organs.
items.reason: Imaging and biopsies can often miss scattered granulomata, making diagnosis challenging without evidence from other organs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why biopsy-proven sarcoidosis of other organs aids in diagnosing cardiac sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Sarcoidosis is most commonly diagnosed in individuals over the age of 50.
items.reason: The passage states that sarcoidosis typically affects individuals between 30 and 50 years old, often after 50 in women.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because sarcoidosis most commonly affects those between 30 and 50 years old, not primarily over the age of 50.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Myocarditis · NONINFECTIVE INFLAMMATORY MYOCARDITIS (part 1)
